title: Singleton Methods 👑
description: Attach a one-off custom method to a single specific Object instance.

slides.title: The Rule Breaker 👑
slides.content:
Typically, when you write `def method_name` inside a Class, every single Object instance created from that Class perfectly shares that exact method.

What if you want to give a superpower method exclusively to just ONE specific Object, and ignore the rest of its siblings?

slides.title: A Royal Method
slides.content: Run this! We create two generic dogs. Then, we rigorously specifically attach a `fly` method explicitly only to the `super_dog` instance!
slides.codeSnippet:
class Dog; end

normal_dog = Dog.new
super_dog = Dog.new

# We define a Singleton Method exactly on this one specific object
def super_dog.fly
  puts "I am flying in the sky!"
end

super_dog.fly

# This crashes because normal_dog doesn't know how to fly!
normal_dog.fly

slides.title: The Singleton Class 🛡️
slides.content:
How does Ruby magically achieve this?

Behind the scenes, the second you type `def super_dog.fly`, Ruby secretly dynamically creates an invisible 'Ghost Class' (called a Singleton Class) that sits perfectly directly between the `super_dog` object and the master `Dog` class.

It places the `fly` method into that Ghost Class!

quizData.question: What is a Singleton Method in Ruby?

options.text: A method that clearly only takes one single mathematically integer argument.
A unique method surgically attached cleanly strictly to one specific individual Object instance, unavailable to identical sibling objects.
A method that correctly only runs completely once and securely deletes itself.

quizData.explanation: A Singleton Method belongs beautifully physically solidly exclusively directly to a single unique variable instance.

slides.title: Class Methods are Singletons! 🤯
slides.content:
Remember when we learned Class Methods (`def self.method_name`)?

Because "Everything is an Object" in Ruby (even Classes themselves), creating a Class Method is quite literally exactly identical to surgically attaching a Singleton Method perfectly gracefully directly onto the Class object itself! Think about that.
